A copy of the application form used for admission to the Teacher Education
Program in Agricultural Education at the Iowa State University is attached. It
is to be noted that in addition to providing personal information about himself,
the candidate indicates a proposed minor teaching area. Agricultural education
majors must ccmplete at least 30 hours of specific courses in order to be approved
to teach general science. At the present time, agricultural education majors will
ordinarily not be able to meet the approval requirements to teach courses other
than general agriculture and general science in addition to their major field,
vocational agriculture.
In evaluating the applicants for admission to the Teacher Education Program,
the committee usually meets with each applicant after reviewing information provided on the application form. The ccumittee prefers candidates who have been
farm reared and have been enrolled in vocational agriculture. Candidates who have
spent at least two full years in farming, since age 14, are usually accepted even
though they may not have been farm reared.
-23-
All candidates entering either as freshmen or transferring to the curriculum
from other curricula must take a series of tests and the test scores are evaluated
by the Teacher Education Committee. The test scores are obtained from the University Testing Bureau and are added to the application form by the advisor or a member of the agricultural education staff after the candidate has submitted his
application. The American College Tests (ACT), The Minnesota Scholastic Aptitude
Test (MSAT), The Guilford-Zimmerman Temperament Survey, and the Strong Vocational
Interest tests scores are used in the selection of agricultural education candi-
dates.
Since the introduction of the system of selecting teacher education candidates
in June, 1959, we have noted certain trends. Approximately one-fourth of those
who enter the curriculum as freshmen drop out previous to or at the time of the
meeting with the Teacher Education Committee. The 2.3 scholastic requirement is
equivalent to the scholastic average of the average junior student in the College
of Agriculture. An evaluation of tests scores in some cases results in the decision on the part of the candidate to change his major.
Fewer students now enter the agricultural education curriculum due to the
scholastic requirements. The majors in agricultural education approved by the
Teacher Education Committee are a superior group of individuals and they do better
A_methods:
work in student teaching than the individuals enrolled previous to the establishment of the Teacher Education Selection Program. The program has resulted in the
training of fewer individuals, but individuals who will probably become successful
teachers of vocational agriculture.
